volumeTitle: Stichus. Trinummus. Truculentus. Tale of a Travelling Bag. Fragments
label: Truculentus (Section 1.2.145 - Section 2.1.215)

sections.label: Section 1.2.145
sections.original: Plerique idem quod tu facis faciunt rei male gerentes :
sections.translation: Most persons who manage their property badly , do the same as you are doing ; when they haven ’ t wherewith to pay the tax , they blame the farmers of the taxes .

sections.label: Section 1.2.147
sections.original: Male vertit res pecuaria mihi apud vos : nunc vicissim
sections.translation: My pasturage contract with you turns out but badly ; now in its turn , I wish to have , according to my narrow circumstances , a little bit of arable land here with you .

sections.label: Section 1.2.149
sections.original: Non arvos hic , sed pascuost ager : si arationes
sections.translation: Here is no arable , but the field is pasture land . If

sections.label: Section 1.2.150
sections.original: habituru'shabiturus es , quí arari solent , ad pueros ire meliust .
sections.translation: you desire some ploughing , you had better go to those [*] who are in the habit of ploughing ; we hold this public emolument , the right of pasturage ; those are farmers of other taxes .

sections.label: Section 1.2.152
sections.original: Vtrosque pergnovi probe .
sections.translation: Full well enough do I know both sides .

sections.label: Section 1.2.152b
sections.original: Em istoc pol tu otiosu'sotiosus es ,
sections.translation: I ’ troth , it ’ s that way you are a gentleman at ease , since you ’ ve been going wrong both in that direction and in this . But the acquaintance of which do you like the best ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.154
sections.original: Procaciores estis vos , sed illí periuriores ;
sections.translation: You are the more exacting , but they are perjured .

sections.label: Section 1.2.155
sections.original: illis perit quidquid datur , neque ipsís apparet quicquam :
sections.translation: Whatever ’ s given to them is lost outright , nor with themselves is there any show at all of it ; you , if you gain anything , do at least drink and feast it away . In short , they are unprincipled ; you are good-for-nothings , and full of airs .

sections.label: Section 1.2.158
sections.original: Male quae in nos vis , ea omnia tibi dicis , Diniarche ,
sections.translation: All this abuse which , Dinarchus , you are uttering against us and them , you utter against yourself , both as respects us and them .

sections.label: Section 1.2.159b
sections.original: Qui istuc ?
sections.translation: How ’ s that ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.159c
sections.original: Rationem dicam :
sections.translation: I ’ ll tell the reason ;

sections.label: Section 1.2.160
sections.original: quia qui alterum incusat probri , sumpse enitere oportet .
sections.translation: because he who accuses another of dishonesty , him it behoves to look into himself . You who are so prudent , have got nothing from us ; we , who are good-for-nothings , have got all out of you .

sections.label: Section 1.2.162
sections.original: O Ástaphium , haud istoc modo solita es me ante appellare ,
sections.translation: O Astaphium ! you were not in the habit of speaking to me in that fashion formerly , but courteously , when I myself possessed that which is now in your possession .

sections.label: Section 1.2.164
sections.original: Dum vivit , hominem noveris : ubi mortuost , quiescat .
sections.translation: While he ’ s alive , you may know a person ; when he ’ s dead , you may keep yourself quiet .

sections.label: Section 1.2.165
sections.original: te dum vivebas noveram .
sections.translation: I used to know you when you were alive .

sections.label: Section 1.2.165b
sections.original: An me mortuom arbitrare ?
sections.translation: Do you consider me to be dead ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.166
sections.original: Qui potis est , amabo , planius ? qui antehac amator summus
sections.translation: Prithee , how can it be plainer ? He who formerly was esteemed a first-rate lover , for him to be bringing to his mistress nought but lamentations [*] .

sections.label: Section 1.2.168
sections.original: Vestra hercle factum iniuria , quae properavistis olim :
sections.translation: I ’ faith , through your own faults it was done , who in former days were in haste to plunder me . You ought to have done it leisurely , that , unscathed , I might last the longer for you .

sections.label: Section 1.2.170
sections.original: Amator similest oppidi hostilis .
sections.translation: A lover is like an enemy ’ s fortress .

sections.label: Section 1.2.170b
sections.original: Quo argumento est ?
sections.translation: On what ground ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.171
sections.original: Quam primum expugnari potis est , tam id optimum est amicae .
sections.translation: The sooner the lover can be taken by storm , the better it is for the mistress .

sections.label: Section 1.2.172
sections.original: Ego fateor , sed longe aliter est amicus atque amator :
sections.translation: I confess it ; but far different is the friend from the lover . I ’ faith , for sure , the oldest friend ’ s the best one possible for a man .

sections.label: Section 1.2.174b
sections.original: Non hercle occidi , sunt mi etiam fundi et aedis .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , my lands and tenements are not yet all gone .

sections.label: Section 1.2.175
sections.original: Cur , obsecro , ergo ante ostium pro ignoto alienoque astas ?
sections.translation: Why then , prithee , are you standing before the door as a stranger and an alien ? Do go in-doors . Really you are no stranger ; for , upon my faith , not one person this day does she more love in her heart and soul — ( aside ) if , indeed , you ’ ve got land and tenements .

sections.label: Section 1.2.178
sections.original: In melle sunt linguae sitae vostrae atque orationes ,
sections.translation: Your tongues and talk are steeped in honey ; your doings and dispositions are steeped in gall and sour vinegar .

sections.label: Section 1.2.180
sections.original: eo dicta lingua dulcia datis , córde amara facitis .
sections.translation: From your tongues you utter sweet words ; you make your lovers of bitter heart if any don ’ t give you presents .

sections.label: Section 1.2.181
sections.original: Amantes si qui non danunt , † non didici fabulari .
sections.translation: I ’ ve not learnt to say what ’ s false .

sections.label: Section 1.2.182
sections.original: non istaec , mea benignitas , decuit te fabulari ,
sections.translation: It was not this liberality of mine that taught you to say what ’ s false , but those niggardly fellows who are struggling against their appetites .

sections.label: Section 1.2.184
sections.original: Mala es atque eadem quae soles inlecebra .
sections.translation: You are a sly one , and the same artful coaxer that you used to be .

sections.label: Section 1.2.184b
sections.original: Vt exspectatus
sections.translation: How ardently longed for

sections.label: Section 1.2.185
sections.original: peregre advenisti , quam , obsecro , cupiebat te era videre .
sections.translation: have you returned from abroad ! But , prithee , do come , my mistress wants to see you .

sections.label: Section 1.2.186
sections.original: Quid tandem ?
sections.translation: How so , pray ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.186b
sections.original: Te unum ex omnibus amat .
sections.translation: You alone of all mankind does she love .

sections.label: Section 1.2.186c
sections.original: Euge , fundi et aedis ,
sections.translation: Well done , lands and tenements ; you have come to my aid in good time . ( To ASTAPHIUM .) But how say you , Astaphium —?

sections.label: Section 1.2.187b
sections.original: Quid vis ?
sections.translation: What do you want ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.188
sections.original: Estne intus nunc Phronesium ?
sections.translation: Is Phronesium in-doors just now ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.188b
sections.original: Vtut aliis , tibi quidem intus .
sections.translation: To you at all events she ’ s in-doors .

sections.label: Section 1.2.189
sections.original: Valetne ?
sections.translation: Is she well ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.189b
sections.original: Immo edepol melius iam fore spero , te ubi videbit .
sections.translation: Aye , faith , and I do believe she ’ ll be still better when she sees you .

sections.label: Section 1.2.190
sections.original: Hoc nobis vitium maxumumst , cum amamus tum hoc perimus :
sections.translation: This is our greatest fault : when we ’ re in love , then we are undone ; if that which we wish is told us , when manifestly they are telling lies , in our folly we believe it to be true ; verily as though with a tide we fluctuate .

sections.label: Section 1.2.193
sections.original: Heia , haud itast res .
sections.translation: Heyday now — such is not the fact .

sections.label: Section 1.2.193b
sections.original: Ain tu eam me amare ?
sections.translation: Do you say that she loves me ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.193c
sections.original: Immo unice unum .
sections.translation: Aye , you only , alone .

sections.label: Section 1.2.194
sections.original: Peperisse audivi .
sections.translation: I heard that she was brought to bed .

sections.label: Section 1.2.194b
sections.original: Ah obsecro , tace , Diniarche .
sections.translation: Oh , prithee , Dinarchus , do hold your tongue .

sections.label: Section 1.2.194c
sections.original: Quid iam ?
sections.translation: Why so ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.195
sections.original: Horresco misera , mentio quotiens fit partionis ,
sections.translation: I shudder in my alarm , as often as mention is made of childbirth , with such difficulty has Phronesium survived for you . Prithee , do come in-doors now ; do go to see her and wait there a little . She ’ ll be out just now ; for she was at the bath .

sections.label: Section 1.2.198
sections.original: Quid ais tu ? quae numquam fuit praegnas , qui parere potuit ?
sections.translation: What do you say ? She who was never pregnant , how could she be brought to bed ? For really , I never , that I am aware of , perceived her to be in a breeding state .

sections.label: Section 1.2.200
sections.original: Celabat metuebatque te , ne tu sibi persuaderes ,
sections.translation: She concealed it from you and was afraid , lest you should persuade her to have recourse to abortion [*] , and so destroy the child .

sections.label: Section 1.2.202
sections.original: Tum pol isti ést puero pater Babyloniensis miles ,
sections.translation: Troth then , who ’ s the father of this child ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.203
sections.original: quoius núnc ista adventum expetit .
sections.translation: A Babylonian Captain , [*] whose arrival she is now expecting .

sections.label: Section 1.2.203b
sections.original: Immo ab eo ut nuntiatumst ,
sections.translation: So much so , indeed , that , according as was reported , they say that he ’ ll be here just now . I wonder he has not arrived by this .

sections.label: Section 1.2.205
sections.original: Ibo igitur intro ?
sections.translation: Shall I go in , then ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.205b
sections.original: Quippini ? tam audacter quam domum ad te ;
sections.translation: Why not ? As boldly as at home , into your own house ; for even still are you now one of us , Dinarchus .

sections.label: Section 1.2.207
sections.original: Quam mox te huc recipis ?
sections.translation: How soon are you on your return ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.207b
sections.original: Iam hic ero : propest , profecta quo sum .
sections.translation: I ’ ll be there this instant ; it ’ s close at hand where I was going .

sections.label: Section 1.2.208
sections.original: Redi véro actutum . ego interim hic apud vos opperibor .—
sections.translation: But do return directly ; meanwhile I ’ ll wait for you at your house . ( He goes into the house of PHRONESIUM .)

sections.label: Section 2.1.209
sections.original: Hahahaé , requievi ,
sections.translation: Ha , ha , ha ! I ’ m at rest ,

sections.label: Section 2.1.210
sections.original: quia íntro abiit odiúm meum .
sections.translation: since my plague has gone in-doors ; now , indeed , I shall speak according to my own inclination , freely , as I please . My mistress has sung a funeral dirge [*] at our house for this fellow , her lover , over his estate ; for his lands and tenements are mortgaged for his treats in his amour .

sections.label: Section 2.1.215
sections.original: verum apud hunc mea era sua consilia summa eloquitur libere ,
sections.translation: But with him does my mistress speak freely upon the objects of her plans , and so he is rather a friend by way of counsel to her than by way of maintenance . While he had it , he gave ; now he has got nothing ; what he did have , we have got ; what we had , he has now got the same . The common course of things has happened . Fortunes are wont to change upon the instant . Life is checquered .
